summary: McKenzie documents a failed $600 Christmas marketing experiment where he created a mini-site to give away free Christmas bingo cards, hoping parents would link to it and boost his SEO, driving teacher customers to his paid product. The experiment failed on multiple fronts: PDFs acted as 'flypaper' trapping users who never interacted further with the site, the SEO timeline was impossibly aggressive for a seasonal promotion, and paid advertising on StumbleUpon and AdWords generated traffic but no valuable links or conversions. He recovered roughly $50 in marginal sales against $600 in costs, nearly producing his first monthly loss. The post-mortem extracts six concrete lessons for future iterations.
key_points: PDFs are 'flypaper' for non-technical users: once they get the download, all site interaction ceases, making PDFs terrible for marketing funnels that depend on further engagement
Time-sensitive promotions and SEO are fundamentally incompatible because new sites take months to rank, not weeks
A high AdWords click-through rate (11%) on a free offering is actually a disaster — you're paying to give things away efficiently
StumbleUpon traffic is essentially worthless for conversion: 5 cents buys 2 seconds of attention from someone browsing channels, not someone with intent
Giving away too much eliminates the market segmentation that drives conversions — the 32-card giveaway removed any reason for core customers to upgrade
money_quotes: PDFs aren't for whitepapers, they are for flypapers.
If a non-technical user enters a PDF, they overwhelmingly do not exit.

I did it so darn well. You want to know the recipe for a high click-through rate on an AdWords ad? ... Blam! 11% click through rate to a paid listing. Which means Google gives me the top spot, and I get to have the maximum opportunity to give my money away for nothing.

When someone searches for 'christmas bingo cards' and then clicks on a link to christmasbingocards.com, you know what they are looking for? Christmas bingo cards. That blazingly, achingly clear intent is present in no other search mediums.
Paying nothing for traffic of no value is still not a good deal.
Stumbleupon: channel surfing for the Internet… or ADHD with an API?
Funny how things look cheap when you're buying them in 8 cent increments but expensive when you're buying them in $40 increments, even when the 8 cent promotion method is ultimately more costly.
Next time, use freelancers, they're cheaper than I am.
key_insight: Marketing experiments fail when you optimize individual tactics (high CTR, free content, paid traffic) without a coherent funnel connecting attention to conversion, especially when your content format and timeline work against you.
